title: OOP & Classes
subtitle: Same principles, explicit self, and multiple inheritance

sections.java: // Fields, constructor, getters, equals... public class User { private final String name; private int age; public User(String name, int age) { this.name = name; this.age = age; } public String getName() { return name; } }
sections.python: # self is explicit, __init__ is the constructor class User: def __init__(self, name: str, age: int): self.name = name self.age = age # No getters needed — attributes are public def greet(self) -> str: return f"Hi, {self.name}"

sections.title: self Is Explicit — and You'll Forget It
sections.content: Every instance method takes self as its first parameter (Java's implicit this). You write it in the definition but not at the call site: user.greet(). Forgetting self in a method definition is the most common beginner error. Access fields as self.name — bare name won't find the attribute.

sections.java: // A whole data class, by hand or via record public record Point(int x, int y) {} // record gives constructor, equals, // hashCode, toString for free
sections.python: # @dataclass is Python's record from dataclasses import dataclass @dataclass class Point: x: int y: int # __init__, __eq__, __repr__ generated

sections.title: @dataclass = Java record, but Mutable by Default
sections.content: @dataclass generates __init__, __eq__, and __repr__ from your annotated fields — like a Java record. Unlike a record it's mutable unless you pass @dataclass(frozen=True). Add defaults, methods, and field(default_factory=list) for safe mutable defaults.

sections.java: // Single inheritance + interfaces public class Admin extends User implements Auditable { public Admin(String n, int a) { super(n, a); } @Override public String greet() { return "Admin " + getName(); } }
sections.python: # Inheritance in parentheses, super() too class Admin(User): def __init__(self, name: str, age: int): super().__init__(name, age) # No @Override keyword — just redefine def greet(self) -> str: return f"Admin {self.name}"

sections.title: Encapsulation Is by Convention
sections.content: Python has no private keyword. The convention: a single underscore _internal means "don't touch" (protected-ish), and a double underscore __secret triggers name-mangling to avoid subclass collisions. Nothing is truly hidden — Python trusts the developer. Use @property when you need getter/setter logic.

tabs.label: Duck typing
tabs.content:
# No interface needed — if it has .read(), it works
def load(source) -> str:
    return source.read()   # any object with read()

load(open("f.txt"))         # a file
load(io.StringIO("data"))   # an in-memory buffer

In Java you'd declare a Readable interface and every type would implements it. Python only asks: does the object have the method? "If it walks like a duck..."

tabs.label: Protocol (static)
tabs.content:
from typing import Protocol

class Readable(Protocol):
    def read(self) -> str: ...

def load(source: Readable) -> str:
    return source.read()

Protocol gives static, structural typing: mypy checks the shape, but classes never declare implements Readable — they just match it. Java interfaces, inverted.

tabs.label: Objects all the way
tabs.content:
# Classes, functions, modules are all objects
handlers = {"json": parse_json, "csv": parse_csv}
parser = handlers[fmt]          # a function in a dict

# Monkey-patching: rebind attributes at runtime
import service
service.now = lambda: fixed_time   # e.g. swap in a test

There's no static/final wall. Functions, classes, and modules are first-class objects you can pass around, store, and replace — powerful, and occasionally dangerous.

sections.question: Which statement about `self` in Python is correct?
options: self is a reserved keyword like Java's this
self is passed automatically, so you omit it in method definitions
self must be the first parameter of instance methods but is omitted at the call site
sections.explanation: self is an ordinary parameter (not a keyword) that, by convention, is the first parameter of every instance method. Python passes the instance to it automatically when you call obj.method(), so you write self in the definition but never at the call site. It's required in every instance method, not just __init__.
